text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is one of the most common gastrointestinal disorders in the world today, with a prevalence of three hundred sixty over one hundred thousand people. For the majority of patients, this is a self-limited condition that may be improved with changes in lifestyle or medical therapy. However, twenty-five percent of patients with GERD will develop progressive disease that does not respond to simple therapy. Some of these patients may benefit from anti-reflex procedure. Some basic requirements for consideration for an anti-reflex procedure include complications of esophageal acid exposure such as stricture, Barrett's esophagus, and the presence of alkaline reflux. Other indications include persistent symptoms after twelve weeks of medical therapy and the inability to continue medical therapy. We believe that most of the patients should have the following tests prior to the surgery. EGD with biopsies, esophageal manometry, twenty-four hours pH study. This is not required if the patient has atypical history of gastroesophageal reflux. and a barium swallow.

The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with the head elevated at a 25-degree angle. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, while the camera operator is on the right and the assistant on the left. Four trocars are inserted into the abdomen, with the laparoscope introduced through a midline port above the umbilicus. The surgeon begins mobilizing the gastric fundus by dissecting the gastroesplenic ligament and dividing the cephalic short gastric vessels, revealing a small fat pad at the stomach's end. The posterior attachments of the upper gastric fundus are divided, opening the rear cavity.

The surgeon identifies the posterior window and dissects a space superior and medial to the free edge of the right crus to facilitate closure of the hiatus. The esophagus is retracted anteriorly and to the left, while the crura are approximated starting at the level of the aortic dissection.

The surgeon passes a forceps behind the esophagus from right to left, grasping the posterior wall of the gastric fundus to form the wrap. A 45 French bougie is inserted to calibrate the fundoplication, followed by placing stitches to secure the wrap, which measures approximately five centimeters in total length.

After these procedures, patients are allowed to take liquids on recovery from anesthesia and are maintained on a diet of pureed food for one to two weeks. Thereafter, they can begin to take soft foods and go on to a normal diet after four to six weeks. Light activity is encouraged and heavy lifting is avoided for four weeks. Analgesia is given in liquid form for the first two weeks and all pills are crushed. Most patients are allowed to leave the hospital on the first post-operative day.
